description: This figure presents a dual-axis scatter plot comparing the mechanical property balance of an Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel and various reference alloys. The horizontal axis shows yield strength (R_p0.2) ranging from approximately 300 to 1000 MPa. The left vertical axis (blue) reports Charpy impact energy in J/cm², while the right vertical axis (red) reports total elongation in %. Green stars, positioned at approximately 480 MPa / 130 J/cm², 580 MPa / 115 J/cm², and 650 MPa / 105 J/cm², represent the present steel after hot-rolling reductions of 45%, 68%, and 82%, respectively, showing the progressive increase in yield strength with rolling reduction. Blue symbols (solid and open circles, triangles, squares, and inverted triangles) denote Charpy impact energy data for comparison materials, and red symbols denote their total elongations. The plot reveals a general strength–ductility/toughness trade-off among reference alloys, including aging-hardened Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys with high yield strength but low elongation (~10–20%) and toughness, and solution-treated austenitic stainless steels or Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys with lower strength but higher ductility. The present steel maintains superior elongation (~50–60%) and impact energy (~100–130 J/cm²) even at elevated yield strengths, supporting the conclusion that increasing hot-rolling reduction achieves an exceptional strength–ductility–toughness synergy primarily through grain refinement and dislocation strengthening.
